full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.84|> It is, as I said last week, wonderful to be back.<|4.84|> <|4.84|> And I'm saying that even after a full week of work.<|8.08|> <|8.08|> So that's a good thing.<|9.98|> <|9.98|> I was a little nervous when Richard wasn't here last week.<|12.32|> <|12.32|> I didn't think that boded well,<|13.84|> <|13.84|> but he came back, so all is well.<|16.58|> <|17.92|> It is wonderful to be back in this beautiful place<|21.06|> <|21.06|> with transcendent music,<|24.40|> <|24.40|> moving prayers, and your gorgeous faces.<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and it's fun to be back in the pulpit.<|2.38|> <|3.76|> I missed you all.<|5.06|> <|5.58|> Three months is a long time to be away.<|7.96|> <|9.10|> Long enough for some of you who were not well to get better,<|13.58|> <|14.40|> and long enough for some to have left us.<|17.28|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is the joy and the sorrow of being part of a tight-knit community, isn't it?<|7.00|> <|7.00|> A lot can happen in three months.<|11.00|> <|11.00|> I've shared with many of you that for me it really and truly was a time of rest,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> even if new puppy Hattie made some nights not particularly restful.<|24.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And it was a time of deep reconnection.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Like Richard, during his sabbatical, I took the opportunity very intentionally to be with my family and to be with friends who are like family.<|19.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Friends who have known me my entire adult life.<|5.54|> <|5.54|> Friends who stood witness for Dawn and me when we made our wedding vows 32 years ago.<|14.30|> <|14.30|> Friends who were with me at my ordinations.<|18.74|> <|18.74|> Friends who knew my mother.<|22.34|> <|22.34|> Friends who love our children.<|26.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The kind of friends who can be very rare and precious when you spend so much of your adult<|8.98|> <|8.98|> life moving from place to place.<|13.50|> <|13.50|> Friends who are like family.<|17.20|> <|17.20|> Or as we say at my house, family without the baggage.<|24.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.24|> After this time away and spending so much of it with these precious folks,<|10.24|> <|11.20|> I couldn't help but ponder this idea of found family,<|17.60|> <|18.24|> or as sociologists and anthropologists call it, chosen family.<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> when I was reflecting on the idea of adoption in today's text from Paul's letter to the Romans.<|7.98|> <|9.46|> The experts who study family structures and systems see chosen family fulfilling many needs.<|21.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Perhaps it is a way to connect deeply with people when you are geographically separated from your family of origin.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> Or some may choose family figures to replace a role vacated by a too soon lost parent.<|21.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I've been blessed to have a number of women step in this enormous space that was left by my mother's death.<|10.48|> <|11.94|> Or often, choosing family is a way to heal wounds and trauma that may have been inflicted by our biological families.<|25.72|> <|25.72|> Amen.<|26.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, too many of our LGBTQ siblings know this to be true.<|7.40|> <|7.40|> Chosen family are often the friends who love us unconditionally.<|15.68|> <|15.68|> They often share many of our deepest beliefs,<|20.60|> <|20.60|> they invite us to grow,<|23.12|> <|23.12|> and they show up for us in all aspects of life.<|27.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We adopt people as family, don't we?<|7.00|> <|7.00|> Interestingly, well interestingly to me, the ancient Hebrew people had no language for this.<|16.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They had no word for adoption.<|4.54|> <|4.54|> It was not until the 20th century that the Hebrew language took on adopted a word for<|12.50|> <|12.50|> adoption.<|13.50|> <|13.50|> So, it's no surprise that there was no notion in Hebrew scripture that family could extend<|21.44|> <|21.44|> beyond the biological, not formally anyway.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Bloodlines were paramount to belonging.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Jesus had a few things to say about that.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> There are many who shy away from using family language regarding church.<|18.00|> <|18.00|> Family language can certainly be freighted, and family systems can certainly be messy.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But personally, I don't know how we avoid it if we really listen to Jesus.<|6.82|> <|8.54|> Jesus himself tells his mother and brothers when they are an obstacle to his ministry<|15.92|> <|15.92|> that they are no longer his family.<|18.44|> <|19.24|> His followers are his family.<|21.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> From the cross, Jesus united the beloved disciple and his mother Mary as mother and son.<|8.20|> <|9.90|> Jesus constantly used familial language to talk about and to his followers, brothers and sisters, siblings.<|21.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thanks.<|0.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As Jesus follower and a missionary, Paul had to look for ways to connect with the culture and the people he was evangelizing.<|16.50|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How can he understand them so that they can understand the gospel?<|7.38|> <|8.24|> This is the challenge for every missionary throughout time.<|12.14|> <|13.26|> And it reminds me of one of the most remarkable stories in missiology, that study of the missionary work of the church.<|22.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There was a Canadian missionary named Don Richardson, and he went to live among, wait<|6.96|> <|6.96|> for it, headhunters in Indonesia in the 1960s.<|14.06|> <|14.06|> He was having no luck at all connecting the locals to the story of Jesus.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He learned their language, he spent time with them, he could not figure out a way in until he realized that culturally the people of that area related to Judas.<|18.30|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To them, Judas represented some of their cultural norms, and he was actually the hero of the<|8.28|> <|8.28|> stories they were hearing, not Jesus.<|10.82|> <|12.20|> Crazy, right?<|13.08|> <|15.46|> So Don Richardson backed into the gospel message using Judas as the entry point of<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> until he could get them to understand that Jesus was the hero of the story.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> There's an amazing book called Peace Child that is an account of his time in Indonesia<|13.00|> <|13.00|> and his missionary achievement in spreading the good news of Christ to a remote corner of the world in a most unusual way.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So that is what Paul has to do as well.<|6.64|> <|6.64|> Paul the missionary, he went not to headhunters, but to Gentiles, the Romans.<|14.74|> <|14.74|> How could he reach them?<|16.82|> <|16.82|> How could he get them to understand the good news of Jesus and that it was also meant for<|24.20|> <|24.20|> them?<|25.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How could he get them an urban people, non-Jewish, so far away from the dusty paths that Jesus trod?<|10.92|> <|11.64|> How could Paul reach them?<|14.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Paul had to convince the Romans that they did not first need to become Jewish before becoming followers of Christ.<|8.16|> <|9.24|> He had to convince them that they too could be co-inheritors of God's promises,<|15.24|> <|15.80|> even when they were not children of the covenant,<|18.30|> <|19.56|> even when they had no traceable lineage to Abraham.<|24.38|> <|25.66|> How could they as Gentiles?<|28.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> My friends, how can we?<|6.46|> <|6.46|> Unlike Israel, adoption was an important part of ancient Roman culture.<|14.14|> <|14.14|> Wealthy families practiced adoption, sometimes even adopting adult men to guarantee succession<|22.38|> <|22.38|> if there was no male heir.<|24.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I want to disavow you of anything you may be thinking about our common current idea of adopting vulnerable babies and small children.<|12.36|> <|14.18|> These adopted children...<|17.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We're privy to all of the rights and privileges of biological children, and even more, because<|8.04|> <|8.04|> a biological child could be disowned, and by law, an adopted child could not.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In ancient Roman culture, an adopted child received a new identity.<|7.50|> <|7.50|> Any prior commitments, responsibilities, and debts were canceled.<|13.54|> <|13.54|> New rights and responsibilities were taken on.<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Also in ancient Rome, the concept of inheritance was a part of life, not something that began at death.<|11.00|> <|12.00|> Being adopted made someone an heir to their father, a joint sharer in all possessions, and fully united to the parent.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.10|> Does that sound familiar?<|2.36|> <|3.84|> Sounds like a pretty solid entry point to me.<|7.42|> <|8.98|> Chosen family was already a vital part<|13.98|> <|14.36|> of the familial fabric of Roman society.<|17.72|> <|18.88|> Adoption was the perfect way for Paul to explain<|23.42|> <|23.42|> to the Romans what it meant to have a life in Christ.<|27.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> a fully welcomed and fully participatory life in Christ.<|6.94|> <|6.94|> Family language permeates this passage.<|11.94|> <|11.94|> Brothers and sisters, siblings,<|15.82|> <|15.82|> Abba, father, heirs, children, adoption.<|21.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How does that resonate with you?<|5.94|> <|5.94|> What does it mean to you to be adopted by God, to be a co-inheritor with Christ, a member<|16.70|> <|16.70|> of the family of Christ?<|19.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What benefits does this chosen family of God, this chosen family of St. John's, impart?<|10.00|> <|10.00|> What difference does it make?<|14.00|> <|14.00|> I know it has made all the difference in my life.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through adoption, we are all children of God.<|6.16|> <|6.16|> Through adoption, we are joint heirs with Christ.<|10.40|> <|10.72|> Through the waters of baptism, we are buried with him in his death, and we share in his resurrection.<|17.14|> <|18.66|> Through adoption, we all have hope for what we do not, what we cannot yet see.<|28.18|> <|28.18|> Amen.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Through adoption, my friends, we have found each other.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> We are a chosen family.<|13.52|> <|13.52|> Thanks be to God.<|14.36|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|2.00|><|endoftext|>
